######  【13†Blog】 > 【26†News & Analysis】  | November 4, 2022 

###### TAGS: 【27†automation】 | 【28†Kubernetes】

# Hedgehog, Kubernetes, And The Network Automation Conundrum

###### 【29†Drew Conry-Murray】

This post originally appeared in Human Infrastructure, the Packet Pushers’ weekly newsletter. See 【30†back issues and sign up here】 to get it.

* * *

The networking startup 【31†Hedgehog†githedgehog.com】 recently emerged from stealth with a network fabric that brings together the open-source 【32†SONiC†github.com】 network OS (NOS) and the Kubernetes orchestration platform. The goal is to provide a distributed L3 network fabric (think edge, colocation, and cloud deployments) that can run on whitebox switches, SmartNICs/DPUs, and CPUs.

While Hedgehog is building its solution around SONiC and Kubernetes, the real value proposition is what these two elements aim to make possible: a network fabric that’s designed to be adaptable, software-driven, and automation-friendly. And because Hedgehog’s software can be deployed on a variety of hardware types, the startup can bring network connectivity to edge locations, data centers, and the cloud.

## Why Kubernetes?

It’s easy to understand why Hedgehog would choose SONiC for its NOS. As an open-source project it’s freely available to Hedgehog. More importantly, SONiC comes with robust community support from heavy hitters such as Microsoft and NVIDIA. Microsoft originally developed SONiC for use in its Azure cloud, and NVIDIA 【33†offers†www.nvidia.com】 switches and DPUs that can run the NOS.

So why throw Kubernetes into the mix? For one, SONiC is a containerized NOS. Individual elements of the NOS run as 【34†Docker containers†infohub.delltechnologies.com】 (for instance, there’s an SNMP container, a BGP container, and so on). Kubernetes is a container orchestration platform, so there’s a natural fit using Kubernetes to orchestrate your distributed SONiC nodes.

For another, Hedgehog is targeting organizations that are embracing modern development and operations practices, such as Web-scale companies, financial services, and very large enterprises. These organizations may already be using Kubernetes to run applications and services. Hedgehog aims to slot right into these organizations’ existing tools and processes.

“We turn Kubernetes into a network controller,” said Mark Austin, CEO and co-founder, in an interview. “We give customers a tool they can use to design a Hedgehog data center that then drives configuration of Hedgehog SONiC instances. Network functions can run in containers so we can compose or orchestrate a SONiC image, and then automate the configuration of any given network device for its role in the fabric.”

“We want to close the gap between DevOps, SRE, and NetOps,” he said.

Besides the NOS, Hedgehog provides a fabric operator to apply configurations to the SONiC nodes, set up BGP, create rules, and so on. There’s also a fabric designer, which is provided via SaaS. The fabric designer lets customers diagram and model the entire fabric in the cloud–including racks, switches, and DPUs–and then download seed images to the network devices, which will bring up those images and join the Kubernetes cluster.

## The Network Automation Conundrum

There’s a problem for Hedgehog and other networking companies looking to draft off the current enthusiasm for DevOps/NetOps/GitOps, Kubernetes, and the like. That problem is the network automation conundrum.

The 5 minutes you might save via automation requires many, many hours to build a reliable automation process. A reliable automation process is more than just a collection of scripts. You need telemetry and a source of truth. You need testing and code review to make sure the 【35†changes or tasks you hope to automate don’t destroy the network†blog.cloudflare.com】. You need a commit process to track who is making changes and why. You need training on new tools and products. Most importantly, you need buy-in from everyone on the team about all the new processes and pipelines.

This is not to say automation is bad or unfeasible. 【36†It’s not.†blog.ipspace.net】 Over time, the 5-minute jobs you automate add up to significant efficiencies. Even better, the testing and validation processes in your automation pipeline should reduce the problems that get introduced by human error. But it’s hard to get busy engineers to justify the time spent learning and building a reliable automation process when they can clear those blinking-red tasks by hand right now.

Hedgehog shouldn’t be expected to solve this problem. It’s not a problem any vendor can solve. But I’m not convinced you can put a Kubernetes wrapper over networking and then turn the network over to a DevOps team and expect a lot of success–and certainly not when something goes wrong. Kubernetes and DevOps is not a replacement for network engineers who know what they’re doing.

I wonder if Hedgehog is a little ahead of the curve in expecting to find a market of the right people with the right training and processes in place to take advantage of what the startup offers.

2 min read 

# The Benefits of Containers in SONiC

【12† Josh Saul 】 :  Mar 14, 2023 6:56:50 AM 

【13†Uncategorized】 

[Image 2: The Benefits of Containers in SONiC] 

The Benefits of Containers in SONiC - Hedgehog 

4:27

If you’re into network operating systems like me, then you’ll know that the containerization architecture of SONiC is a huge step forward for delivering on higher levels of network uptime.

In the past, most network operating systems were monolithic, which means they had a single codebase controlling all the network functions, one big fat image with all the features. But containerization changes all of that! Now, each network function is separated into its own container or microservice. It’s like having different rooms for different functions in your house instead of having everything in one big room. You can renovate a room completely while life in the other rooms remains unaffected.

Containers, microservices, and Kubernetes are the standards for deploying distributed applications in the cloud, perhaps it is time for networking to catch up? What can we expect with this new paradigm?

# Flexibility

[Image 3] 

Why are containers such a big deal? For starters, it gives us a lot more flexibility. With containerization, we can update or add new features to a specific service without having to change the entire operating system. This is a game-changer for configuring and troubleshooting networks. In the past, if there was a bug or vulnerability in a component that was not part of the data plane, you still needed to reboot the whole system in order to patch it. Sure there were proprietary solutions like ISSU, but they were somewhat unreliable and part of the vendor’s black box. With containers and open source software, we can be assured of which things are dependent on each other. This is a very important part of developing reliable systems.

# Scalability

[Image 4] 

Another big benefit is scalability. With containerization, we can scale individual services as needed instead of having to scale the entire system. This is great news for handling increasing network traffic without breaking the bank. We can scale up each individual container with additional resources, or spin up new identical containers to parallelize workloads to better match the necessary demand.

# Security

[Image 5] 

Security is also a major plus. Containerization provides an added layer of security for network operating systems. Each service is isolated in its own container, so any security breaches or vulnerabilities only affect that specific service, rather than the whole system. And if we find a piece of code that is insecure, we can replace it easily and quickly because we don’t need to replace the entire OS. That’s peace of mind that’s priceless!

# Efficiency

[Image 6] 

Efficiency is also a big selling point. Containerization allows each service to use only the resources it needs, which leads to cost savings, especially in large-scale networks. And the best part is that SONiC’s containerized approach allows for greater openness and interoperability with other systems. In the same way we might throttle inbound traffic to the management interfaces, we should also set limits on the management processes to ensure that they don’t blow up our control plane. The more control we have over system processes the more space we have available for the new and exciting pieces of software that we’ve always wanted to leverage in our network management.

# Orchestration

[Image 7] 

And here’s a bit of magic that might make you think differently about the future of networking. If the NOS is a collection of containers running our network services on nodes, and the network fabric is a group of these worker nodes participating in delivering a specific outcome, isn’t that basically the same thing as a distributed application? (NOD YOUR HEAD)

So can we use Kubernetes to deploy and operate these network services in an extremely intelligent fashion? You bet! We don’t need any proprietary network management system (NMS) or network controller, we can use Kubernetes and get all of the benefits of the entire Cloud Native community of tools to dramatically improve our capabilities.

## This is game changing.

Overall, I think containerization in SONiC is a huge improvement over monolithic network operating systems. It’s a more flexible, scalable, secure, efficient, and open approach. It’s exactly what application developers want when designing modern apps. The time has come for the network to catch up with the rest of modern infrastructure.

Till next time…

[Image 8]

# The Benefits of Containers in SONiC

【12† Josh Saul 】 :  Mar 14, 2023 6:56:50 AM 

【13†Uncategorized】 

[Image 2: The Benefits of Containers in SONiC] 

The Benefits of Containers in SONiC - Hedgehog 

4:27

If you’re into network operating systems like me, then you’ll know that the containerization architecture of SONiC is a huge step forward for delivering on higher levels of network uptime.

In the past, most network operating systems were monolithic, which means they had a single codebase controlling all the network functions, one big fat image with all the features. But containerization changes all of that! Now, each network function is separated into its own container or microservice. It’s like having different rooms for different functions in your house instead of having everything in one big room. You can renovate a room completely while life in the other rooms remains unaffected.

Containers, microservices, and Kubernetes are the standards for deploying distributed applications in the cloud, perhaps it is time for networking to catch up? What can we expect with this new paradigm?

# Flexibility

[Image 3] 

Why are containers such a big deal? For starters, it gives us a lot more flexibility. With containerization, we can update or add new features to a specific service without having to change the entire operating system. This is a game-changer for configuring and troubleshooting networks. In the past, if there was a bug or vulnerability in a component that was not part of the data plane, you still needed to reboot the whole system in order to patch it. Sure there were proprietary solutions like ISSU, but they were somewhat unreliable and part of the vendor’s black box. With containers and open source software, we can be assured of which things are dependent on each other. This is a very important part of developing reliable systems.

# Scalability

[Image 4] 

Another big benefit is scalability. With containerization, we can scale individual services as needed instead of having to scale the entire system. This is great news for handling increasing network traffic without breaking the bank. We can scale up each individual container with additional resources, or spin up new identical containers to parallelize workloads to better match the necessary demand.

# Security

[Image 5] 

Security is also a major plus. Containerization provides an added layer of security for network operating systems. Each service is isolated in its own container, so any security breaches or vulnerabilities only affect that specific service, rather than the whole system. And if we find a piece of code that is insecure, we can replace it easily and quickly because we don’t need to replace the entire OS. That’s peace of mind that’s priceless!

# Efficiency

[Image 6] 

Efficiency is also a big selling point. Containerization allows each service to use only the resources it needs, which leads to cost savings, especially in large-scale networks. And the best part is that SONiC’s containerized approach allows for greater openness and interoperability with other systems. In the same way we might throttle inbound traffic to the management interfaces, we should also set limits on the management processes to ensure that they don’t blow up our control plane. The more control we have over system processes the more space we have available for the new and exciting pieces of software that we’ve always wanted to leverage in our network management.

# Orchestration

[Image 7] 

And here’s a bit of magic that might make you think differently about the future of networking. If the NOS is a collection of containers running our network services on nodes, and the network fabric is a group of these worker nodes participating in delivering a specific outcome, isn’t that basically the same thing as a distributed application? (NOD YOUR HEAD)

So can we use Kubernetes to deploy and operate these network services in an extremely intelligent fashion? You bet! We don’t need any proprietary network management system (NMS) or network controller, we can use Kubernetes and get all of the benefits of the entire Cloud Native community of tools to dramatically improve our capabilities.

## This is game changing.

Overall, I think containerization in SONiC is a huge improvement over monolithic network operating systems. It’s a more flexible, scalable, secure, efficient, and open approach. It’s exactly what application developers want when designing modern apps. The time has come for the network to catch up with the rest of modern infrastructure.

Till next time…

[Image 8]

######  【13†Blog】 > 【26†News & Analysis】  | November 4, 2022 

###### TAGS: 【27†automation】 | 【28†Kubernetes】

# Hedgehog, Kubernetes, And The Network Automation Conundrum

###### 【29†Drew Conry-Murray】

This post originally appeared in Human Infrastructure, the Packet Pushers’ weekly newsletter. See 【30†back issues and sign up here】 to get it.

* * *

The networking startup 【31†Hedgehog†githedgehog.com】 recently emerged from stealth with a network fabric that brings together the open-source 【32†SONiC†github.com】 network OS (NOS) and the Kubernetes orchestration platform. The goal is to provide a distributed L3 network fabric (think edge, colocation, and cloud deployments) that can run on whitebox switches, SmartNICs/DPUs, and CPUs.

While Hedgehog is building its solution around SONiC and Kubernetes, the real value proposition is what these two elements aim to make possible: a network fabric that’s designed to be adaptable, software-driven, and automation-friendly. And because Hedgehog’s software can be deployed on a variety of hardware types, the startup can bring network connectivity to edge locations, data centers, and the cloud.

## Why Kubernetes?

It’s easy to understand why Hedgehog would choose SONiC for its NOS. As an open-source project it’s freely available to Hedgehog. More importantly, SONiC comes with robust community support from heavy hitters such as Microsoft and NVIDIA. Microsoft originally developed SONiC for use in its Azure cloud, and NVIDIA 【33†offers†www.nvidia.com】 switches and DPUs that can run the NOS.

So why throw Kubernetes into the mix? For one, SONiC is a containerized NOS. Individual elements of the NOS run as 【34†Docker containers†infohub.delltechnologies.com】 (for instance, there’s an SNMP container, a BGP container, and so on). Kubernetes is a container orchestration platform, so there’s a natural fit using Kubernetes to orchestrate your distributed SONiC nodes.

For another, Hedgehog is targeting organizations that are embracing modern development and operations practices, such as Web-scale companies, financial services, and very large enterprises. These organizations may already be using Kubernetes to run applications and services. Hedgehog aims to slot right into these organizations’ existing tools and processes.

“We turn Kubernetes into a network controller,” said Mark Austin, CEO and co-founder, in an interview. “We give customers a tool they can use to design a Hedgehog data center that then drives configuration of Hedgehog SONiC instances. Network functions can run in containers so we can compose or orchestrate a SONiC image, and then automate the configuration of any given network device for its role in the fabric.”

“We want to close the gap between DevOps, SRE, and NetOps,” he said.

Besides the NOS, Hedgehog provides a fabric operator to apply configurations to the SONiC nodes, set up BGP, create rules, and so on. There’s also a fabric designer, which is provided via SaaS. The fabric designer lets customers diagram and model the entire fabric in the cloud–including racks, switches, and DPUs–and then download seed images to the network devices, which will bring up those images and join the Kubernetes cluster.

## The Network Automation Conundrum

There’s a problem for Hedgehog and other networking companies looking to draft off the current enthusiasm for DevOps/NetOps/GitOps, Kubernetes, and the like. That problem is the network automation conundrum.

The 5 minutes you might save via automation requires many, many hours to build a reliable automation process. A reliable automation process is more than just a collection of scripts. You need telemetry and a source of truth. You need testing and code review to make sure the 【35†changes or tasks you hope to automate don’t destroy the network†blog.cloudflare.com】. You need a commit process to track who is making changes and why. You need training on new tools and products. Most importantly, you need buy-in from everyone on the team about all the new processes and pipelines.

This is not to say automation is bad or unfeasible. 【36†It’s not.†blog.ipspace.net】 Over time, the 5-minute jobs you automate add up to significant efficiencies. Even better, the testing and validation processes in your automation pipeline should reduce the problems that get introduced by human error. But it’s hard to get busy engineers to justify the time spent learning and building a reliable automation process when they can clear those blinking-red tasks by hand right now.

Hedgehog shouldn’t be expected to solve this problem. It’s not a problem any vendor can solve. But I’m not convinced you can put a Kubernetes wrapper over networking and then turn the network over to a DevOps team and expect a lot of success–and certainly not when something goes wrong. Kubernetes and DevOps is not a replacement for network engineers who know what they’re doing.

I wonder if Hedgehog is a little ahead of the curve in expecting to find a market of the right people with the right training and processes in place to take advantage of what the startup offers.

# Hedgehog, Kubernetes, And The Network Automation Conundrum

###### 【29†Drew Conry-Murray】

This post originally appeared in Human Infrastructure, the Packet Pushers’ weekly newsletter. See 【30†back issues and sign up here】 to get it.

* * *

The networking startup 【31†Hedgehog†githedgehog.com】 recently emerged from stealth with a network fabric that brings together the open-source 【32†SONiC†github.com】 network OS (NOS) and the Kubernetes orchestration platform. The goal is to provide a distributed L3 network fabric (think edge, colocation, and cloud deployments) that can run on whitebox switches, SmartNICs/DPUs, and CPUs.

While Hedgehog is building its solution around SONiC and Kubernetes, the real value proposition is what these two elements aim to make possible: a network fabric that’s designed to be adaptable, software-driven, and automation-friendly. And because Hedgehog’s software can be deployed on a variety of hardware types, the startup can bring network connectivity to edge locations, data centers, and the cloud.

## Why Kubernetes?

It’s easy to understand why Hedgehog would choose SONiC for its NOS. As an open-source project it’s freely available to Hedgehog. More importantly, SONiC comes with robust community support from heavy hitters such as Microsoft and NVIDIA. Microsoft originally developed SONiC for use in its Azure cloud, and NVIDIA 【33†offers†www.nvidia.com】 switches and DPUs that can run the NOS.

So why throw Kubernetes into the mix? For one, SONiC is a containerized NOS. Individual elements of the NOS run as 【34†Docker containers†infohub.delltechnologies.com】 (for instance, there’s an SNMP container, a BGP container, and so on). Kubernetes is a container orchestration platform, so there’s a natural fit using Kubernetes to orchestrate your distributed SONiC nodes.

For another, Hedgehog is targeting organizations that are embracing modern development and operations practices, such as Web-scale companies, financial services, and very large enterprises. These organizations may already be using Kubernetes to run applications and services. Hedgehog aims to slot right into these organizations’ existing tools and processes.

“We turn Kubernetes into a network controller,” said Mark Austin, CEO and co-founder, in an interview. “We give customers a tool they can use to design a Hedgehog data center that then drives configuration of Hedgehog SONiC instances. Network functions can run in containers so we can compose or orchestrate a SONiC image, and then automate the configuration of any given network device for its role in the fabric.”

“We want to close the gap between DevOps, SRE, and NetOps,” he said.

Besides the NOS, Hedgehog provides a fabric operator to apply configurations to the SONiC nodes, set up BGP, create rules, and so on. There’s also a fabric designer, which is provided via SaaS. The fabric designer lets customers diagram and model the entire fabric in the cloud–including racks, switches, and DPUs–and then download seed images to the network devices, which will bring up those images and join the Kubernetes cluster.

## The Network Automation Conundrum

There’s a problem for Hedgehog and other networking companies looking to draft off the current enthusiasm for DevOps/NetOps/GitOps, Kubernetes, and the like. That problem is the network automation conundrum.

The 5 minutes you might save via automation requires many, many hours to build a reliable automation process. A reliable automation process is more than just a collection of scripts. You need telemetry and a source of truth. You need testing and code review to make sure the 【35†changes or tasks you hope to automate don’t destroy the network†blog.cloudflare.com】. You need a commit process to track who is making changes and why. You need training on new tools and products. Most importantly, you need buy-in from everyone on the team about all the new processes and pipelines.

This is not to say automation is bad or unfeasible. 【36†It’s not.†blog.ipspace.net】 Over time, the 5-minute jobs you automate add up to significant efficiencies. Even better, the testing and validation processes in your automation pipeline should reduce the problems that get introduced by human error. But it’s hard to get busy engineers to justify the time spent learning and building a reliable automation process when they can clear those blinking-red tasks by hand right now.

Hedgehog shouldn’t be expected to solve this problem. It’s not a problem any vendor can solve. But I’m not convinced you can put a Kubernetes wrapper over networking and then turn the network over to a DevOps team and expect a lot of success–and certainly not when something goes wrong. Kubernetes and DevOps is not a replacement for network engineers who know what they’re doing.

I wonder if Hedgehog is a little ahead of the curve in expecting to find a market of the right people with the right training and processes in place to take advantage of what the startup offers.

About Drew Conry-Murray: Drew Conry-Murray has been writing about information technology for more than 15 years, with an emphasis on networking, security, and cloud. He's co-host of 【37†The Network Break podcast】 and a Tech Field Day delegate. He loves real tea and virtual donuts, and is delighted that his job lets him talk with so many smart, passionate people. He writes 【38†novels†www.amazon.com】 in his spare time.

# The Benefits of Containers in SONiC

【12† Josh Saul 】 :  Mar 14, 2023 6:56:50 AM 

【13†Uncategorized】 

[Image 2: The Benefits of Containers in SONiC] 

The Benefits of Containers in SONiC - Hedgehog 

4:27

If you’re into network operating systems like me, then you’ll know that the containerization architecture of SONiC is a huge step forward for delivering on higher levels of network uptime.

In the past, most network operating systems were monolithic, which means they had a single codebase controlling all the network functions, one big fat image with all the features. But containerization changes all of that! Now, each network function is separated into its own container or microservice. It’s like having different rooms for different functions in your house instead of having everything in one big room. You can renovate a room completely while life in the other rooms remains unaffected.

Containers, microservices, and Kubernetes are the standards for deploying distributed applications in the cloud, perhaps it is time for networking to catch up? What can we expect with this new paradigm?

# Flexibility

[Image 3] 

Why are containers such a big deal? For starters, it gives us a lot more flexibility. With containerization, we can update or add new features to a specific service without having to change the entire operating system. This is a game-changer for configuring and troubleshooting networks. In the past, if there was a bug or vulnerability in a component that was not part of the data plane, you still needed to reboot the whole system in order to patch it. Sure there were proprietary solutions like ISSU, but they were somewhat unreliable and part of the vendor’s black box. With containers and open source software, we can be assured of which things are dependent on each other. This is a very important part of developing reliable systems.

# Scalability

[Image 4] 

Another big benefit is scalability. With containerization, we can scale individual services as needed instead of having to scale the entire system. This is great news for handling increasing network traffic without breaking the bank. We can scale up each individual container with additional resources, or spin up new identical containers to parallelize workloads to better match the necessary demand.

# Security

[Image 5] 

Security is also a major plus. Containerization provides an added layer of security for network operating systems. Each service is isolated in its own container, so any security breaches or vulnerabilities only affect that specific service, rather than the whole system. And if we find a piece of code that is insecure, we can replace it easily and quickly because we don’t need to replace the entire OS. That’s peace of mind that’s priceless!

# Efficiency

[Image 6] 

Efficiency is also a big selling point. Containerization allows each service to use only the resources it needs, which leads to cost savings, especially in large-scale networks. And the best part is that SONiC’s containerized approach allows for greater openness and interoperability with other systems. In the same way we might throttle inbound traffic to the management interfaces, we should also set limits on the management processes to ensure that they don’t blow up our control plane. The more control we have over system processes the more space we have available for the new and exciting pieces of software that we’ve always wanted to leverage in our network management.

# Orchestration

[Image 7] 

And here’s a bit of magic that might make you think differently about the future of networking. If the NOS is a collection of containers running our network services on nodes, and the network fabric is a group of these worker nodes participating in delivering a specific outcome, isn’t that basically the same thing as a distributed application? (NOD YOUR HEAD)

So can we use Kubernetes to deploy and operate these network services in an extremely intelligent fashion? You bet! We don’t need any proprietary network management system (NMS) or network controller, we can use Kubernetes and get all of the benefits of the entire Cloud Native community of tools to dramatically improve our capabilities.

## This is game changing.

Overall, I think containerization in SONiC is a huge improvement over monolithic network operating systems. It’s a more flexible, scalable, secure, efficient, and open approach. It’s exactly what application developers want when designing modern apps. The time has come for the network to catch up with the rest of modern infrastructure.

Till next time…

[Image 8]

parts: what is a fabric?

In networking, a **fabric** refers to an interwoven structure of network devices that work together to provide connectivity, often across large, distributed systems such as data centers, clouds, or WANs (Wide Area Networks). It is essentially a highly interconnected network architecture that emphasizes scalability, redundancy, and flexibility.

### Key Characteristics of a Network Fabric:
1. **Interconnected Devices**: In a fabric, network devices such as switches and routers are connected in a way that allows traffic to flow between them with minimal latency and bottlenecks.
   
2. **Layer 2 and Layer 3 Networks**: Network fabrics can operate at both Layer 2 (switching) and Layer 3 (routing) of the OSI model. For example, a **Layer 3 fabric** allows for routing decisions based on IP addresses, while a **Layer 2 fabric** handles traffic based on MAC addresses.

3. **Distributed Architecture**: A fabric typically spans across multiple physical or virtual devices to create a single, seamless network that can support large-scale operations. This allows for easier management, higher resiliency, and better scalability.

4. **Automation and Programmability**: Modern network fabrics are often designed with automation in mind, using software-defined networking (SDN) principles. This allows for easier management and scaling through programmable interfaces like APIs.

### Types of Network Fabrics:
- **Data Center Fabric**: Used in large data centers to provide fast and scalable connections between servers, storage, and networking devices. **Spine-and-leaf** architectures are common in data center fabrics to ensure low-latency communication.
- **WAN Fabric**: This refers to the networking architecture that connects geographically dispersed locations, often using SD-WAN (Software-Defined WAN) to provide intelligent routing and optimization.
- **Storage Area Network (SAN) Fabric**: A dedicated fabric used to connect storage devices to servers.

### Benefits:
- **Scalability**: Fabrics allow for easy expansion by adding new devices (like switches or servers) without significantly increasing complexity.
- **Resilience**: Redundant paths between devices ensure that traffic can be rerouted in the case of a failure, making the network highly resilient.
- **Performance**: Fabric designs reduce bottlenecks and enable high-bandwidth, low-latency communication, which is crucial in environments like data centers.

A fabric is, in essence, the underlying architecture that connects various elements in a network, facilitating communication across a scalable, flexible, and resilient infrastructure【10†source】【11†source】.

# Hedgehog, Kubernetes, And The Network Automation Conundrum

###### 【29†Drew Conry-Murray】

This post originally appeared in Human Infrastructure, the Packet Pushers’ weekly newsletter. See 【30†back issues and sign up here】 to get it.

* * *

The networking startup 【31†Hedgehog†githedgehog.com】 recently emerged from stealth with a network fabric that brings together the open-source 【32†SONiC†github.com】 network OS (NOS) and the Kubernetes orchestration platform. The goal is to provide a distributed L3 network fabric (think edge, colocation, and cloud deployments) that can run on whitebox switches, SmartNICs/DPUs, and CPUs.

While Hedgehog is building its solution around SONiC and Kubernetes, the real value proposition is what these two elements aim to make possible: a network fabric that’s designed to be adaptable, software-driven, and automation-friendly. And because Hedgehog’s software can be deployed on a variety of hardware types, the startup can bring network connectivity to edge locations, data centers, and the cloud.

## Why Kubernetes?

It’s easy to understand why Hedgehog would choose SONiC for its NOS. As an open-source project it’s freely available to Hedgehog. More importantly, SONiC comes with robust community support from heavy hitters such as Microsoft and NVIDIA. Microsoft originally developed SONiC for use in its Azure cloud, and NVIDIA 【33†offers†www.nvidia.com】 switches and DPUs that can run the NOS.

So why throw Kubernetes into the mix? For one, SONiC is a containerized NOS. Individual elements of the NOS run as 【34†Docker containers†infohub.delltechnologies.com】 (for instance, there’s an SNMP container, a BGP container, and so on). Kubernetes is a container orchestration platform, so there’s a natural fit using Kubernetes to orchestrate your distributed SONiC nodes.

For another, Hedgehog is targeting organizations that are embracing modern development and operations practices, such as Web-scale companies, financial services, and very large enterprises. These organizations may already be using Kubernetes to run applications and services. Hedgehog aims to slot right into these organizations’ existing tools and processes.

“We turn Kubernetes into a network controller,” said Mark Austin, CEO and co-founder, in an interview. “We give customers a tool they can use to design a Hedgehog data center that then drives configuration of Hedgehog SONiC instances. Network functions can run in containers so we can compose or orchestrate a SONiC image, and then automate the configuration of any given network device for its role in the fabric.”

“We want to close the gap between DevOps, SRE, and NetOps,” he said.

Besides the NOS, Hedgehog provides a fabric operator to apply configurations to the SONiC nodes, set up BGP, create rules, and so on. There’s also a fabric designer, which is provided via SaaS. The fabric designer lets customers diagram and model the entire fabric in the cloud–including racks, switches, and DPUs–and then download seed images to the network devices, which will bring up those images and join the Kubernetes cluster.

## The Network Automation Conundrum

There’s a problem for Hedgehog and other networking companies looking to draft off the current enthusiasm for DevOps/NetOps/GitOps, Kubernetes, and the like. That problem is the network automation conundrum.

The 5 minutes you might save via automation requires many, many hours to build a reliable automation process. A reliable automation process is more than just a collection of scripts. You need telemetry and a source of truth. You need testing and code review to make sure the 【35†changes or tasks you hope to automate don’t destroy the network†blog.cloudflare.com】. You need a commit process to track who is making changes and why. You need training on new tools and products. Most importantly, you need buy-in from everyone on the team about all the new processes and pipelines.

This is not to say automation is bad or unfeasible. 【36†It’s not.†blog.ipspace.net】 Over time, the 5-minute jobs you automate add up to significant efficiencies. Even better, the testing and validation processes in your automation pipeline should reduce the problems that get introduced by human error. But it’s hard to get busy engineers to justify the time spent learning and building a reliable automation process when they can clear those blinking-red tasks by hand right now.

Hedgehog shouldn’t be expected to solve this problem. It’s not a problem any vendor can solve. But I’m not convinced you can put a Kubernetes wrapper over networking and then turn the network over to a DevOps team and expect a lot of success–and certainly not when something goes wrong. Kubernetes and DevOps is not a replacement for network engineers who know what they’re doing.

I wonder if Hedgehog is a little ahead of the curve in expecting to find a market of the right people with the right training and processes in place to take advantage of what the startup offers.

# The Benefits of Containers in SONiC

【12† Josh Saul 】 :  Mar 14, 2023 6:56:50 AM 

【13†Uncategorized】 

[Image 2: The Benefits of Containers in SONiC] 

The Benefits of Containers in SONiC - Hedgehog 

4:27

If you’re into network operating systems like me, then you’ll know that the containerization architecture of SONiC is a huge step forward for delivering on higher levels of network uptime.

In the past, most network operating systems were monolithic, which means they had a single codebase controlling all the network functions, one big fat image with all the features. But containerization changes all of that! Now, each network function is separated into its own container or microservice. It’s like having different rooms for different functions in your house instead of having everything in one big room. You can renovate a room completely while life in the other rooms remains unaffected.

Containers, microservices, and Kubernetes are the standards for deploying distributed applications in the cloud, perhaps it is time for networking to catch up? What can we expect with this new paradigm?

# Flexibility

[Image 3] 

Why are containers such a big deal? For starters, it gives us a lot more flexibility. With containerization, we can update or add new features to a specific service without having to change the entire operating system. This is a game-changer for configuring and troubleshooting networks. In the past, if there was a bug or vulnerability in a component that was not part of the data plane, you still needed to reboot the whole system in order to patch it. Sure there were proprietary solutions like ISSU, but they were somewhat unreliable and part of the vendor’s black box. With containers and open source software, we can be assured of which things are dependent on each other. This is a very important part of developing reliable systems.

# Scalability

[Image 4] 

Another big benefit is scalability. With containerization, we can scale individual services as needed instead of having to scale the entire system. This is great news for handling increasing network traffic without breaking the bank. We can scale up each individual container with additional resources, or spin up new identical containers to parallelize workloads to better match the necessary demand.

# Security

[Image 5] 

Security is also a major plus. Containerization provides an added layer of security for network operating systems. Each service is isolated in its own container, so any security breaches or vulnerabilities only affect that specific service, rather than the whole system. And if we find a piece of code that is insecure, we can replace it easily and quickly because we don’t need to replace the entire OS. That’s peace of mind that’s priceless!

# Efficiency

[Image 6] 

Efficiency is also a big selling point. Containerization allows each service to use only the resources it needs, which leads to cost savings, especially in large-scale networks. And the best part is that SONiC’s containerized approach allows for greater openness and interoperability with other systems. In the same way we might throttle inbound traffic to the management interfaces, we should also set limits on the management processes to ensure that they don’t blow up our control plane. The more control we have over system processes the more space we have available for the new and exciting pieces of software that we’ve always wanted to leverage in our network management.

# Orchestration

[Image 7] 

And here’s a bit of magic that might make you think differently about the future of networking. If the NOS is a collection of containers running our network services on nodes, and the network fabric is a group of these worker nodes participating in delivering a specific outcome, isn’t that basically the same thing as a distributed application? (NOD YOUR HEAD)

So can we use Kubernetes to deploy and operate these network services in an extremely intelligent fashion? You bet! We don’t need any proprietary network management system (NMS) or network controller, we can use Kubernetes and get all of the benefits of the entire Cloud Native community of tools to dramatically improve our capabilities.

## This is game changing.

Overall, I think containerization in SONiC is a huge improvement over monolithic network operating systems. It’s a more flexible, scalable, secure, efficient, and open approach. It’s exactly what application developers want when designing modern apps. The time has come for the network to catch up with the rest of modern infrastructure.

Till next time…

[Image 8]

######  【13†Blog】 > 【26†News & Analysis】  | November 4, 2022 

###### TAGS: 【27†automation】 | 【28†Kubernetes】

# Hedgehog, Kubernetes, And The Network Automation Conundrum

###### 【29†Drew Conry-Murray】

This post originally appeared in Human Infrastructure, the Packet Pushers’ weekly newsletter. See 【30†back issues and sign up here】 to get it.

* * *

The networking startup 【31†Hedgehog†githedgehog.com】 recently emerged from stealth with a network fabric that brings together the open-source 【32†SONiC†github.com】 network OS (NOS) and the Kubernetes orchestration platform. The goal is to provide a distributed L3 network fabric (think edge, colocation, and cloud deployments) that can run on whitebox switches, SmartNICs/DPUs, and CPUs.

While Hedgehog is building its solution around SONiC and Kubernetes, the real value proposition is what these two elements aim to make possible: a network fabric that’s designed to be adaptable, software-driven, and automation-friendly. And because Hedgehog’s software can be deployed on a variety of hardware types, the startup can bring network connectivity to edge locations, data centers, and the cloud.

## Why Kubernetes?

It’s easy to understand why Hedgehog would choose SONiC for its NOS. As an open-source project it’s freely available to Hedgehog. More importantly, SONiC comes with robust community support from heavy hitters such as Microsoft and NVIDIA. Microsoft originally developed SONiC for use in its Azure cloud, and NVIDIA 【33†offers†www.nvidia.com】 switches and DPUs that can run the NOS.

So why throw Kubernetes into the mix? For one, SONiC is a containerized NOS. Individual elements of the NOS run as 【34†Docker containers†infohub.delltechnologies.com】 (for instance, there’s an SNMP container, a BGP container, and so on). Kubernetes is a container orchestration platform, so there’s a natural fit using Kubernetes to orchestrate your distributed SONiC nodes.

For another, Hedgehog is targeting organizations that are embracing modern development and operations practices, such as Web-scale companies, financial services, and very large enterprises. These organizations may already be using Kubernetes to run applications and services. Hedgehog aims to slot right into these organizations’ existing tools and processes.

“We turn Kubernetes into a network controller,” said Mark Austin, CEO and co-founder, in an interview. “We give customers a tool they can use to design a Hedgehog data center that then drives configuration of Hedgehog SONiC instances. Network functions can run in containers so we can compose or orchestrate a SONiC image, and then automate the configuration of any given network device for its role in the fabric.”

“We want to close the gap between DevOps, SRE, and NetOps,” he said.

Besides the NOS, Hedgehog provides a fabric operator to apply configurations to the SONiC nodes, set up BGP, create rules, and so on. There’s also a fabric designer, which is provided via SaaS. The fabric designer lets customers diagram and model the entire fabric in the cloud–including racks, switches, and DPUs–and then download seed images to the network devices, which will bring up those images and join the Kubernetes cluster.

## The Network Automation Conundrum

There’s a problem for Hedgehog and other networking companies looking to draft off the current enthusiasm for DevOps/NetOps/GitOps, Kubernetes, and the like. That problem is the network automation conundrum.

The 5 minutes you might save via automation requires many, many hours to build a reliable automation process. A reliable automation process is more than just a collection of scripts. You need telemetry and a source of truth. You need testing and code review to make sure the 【35†changes or tasks you hope to automate don’t destroy the network†blog.cloudflare.com】. You need a commit process to track who is making changes and why. You need training on new tools and products. Most importantly, you need buy-in from everyone on the team about all the new processes and pipelines.

This is not to say automation is bad or unfeasible. 【36†It’s not.†blog.ipspace.net】 Over time, the 5-minute jobs you automate add up to significant efficiencies. Even better, the testing and validation processes in your automation pipeline should reduce the problems that get introduced by human error. But it’s hard to get busy engineers to justify the time spent learning and building a reliable automation process when they can clear those blinking-red tasks by hand right now.

Hedgehog shouldn’t be expected to solve this problem. It’s not a problem any vendor can solve. But I’m not convinced you can put a Kubernetes wrapper over networking and then turn the network over to a DevOps team and expect a lot of success–and certainly not when something goes wrong. Kubernetes and DevOps is not a replacement for network engineers who know what they’re doing.

I wonder if Hedgehog is a little ahead of the curve in expecting to find a market of the right people with the right training and processes in place to take advantage of what the startup offers.

parts: is it possible to have a fabric that is entirely virtual?

Yes, it is entirely possible to have a **virtual network fabric**, often referred to as a **software-defined fabric** or **overlay network**. These virtual fabrics are created using virtualization and software-defined networking (SDN) technologies, allowing the network to be abstracted from the underlying physical hardware.

### Key Concepts of a Virtual Fabric:
1. **Overlay Networks**: A virtual network fabric operates as an overlay on top of the existing physical network. It uses technologies like **VXLAN (Virtual Extensible LAN)** or **NVGRE (Network Virtualization using Generic Routing Encapsulation)** to encapsulate network traffic and create virtualized layer 2 or layer 3 networks over the physical infrastructure.
   
2. **SDN (Software-Defined Networking)**: SDN decouples the control plane (which decides where traffic should be sent) from the data plane (which forwards the traffic). This allows centralized control and programmability of network functions through software, enabling dynamic and flexible configurations of virtual fabrics.

3. **Virtual Switches and Routers**: Virtual fabrics use **virtualized network devices** (virtual switches, routers, and firewalls) instead of traditional physical devices. These are often deployed as software running on virtual machines or containers, allowing for full network functionality without the need for dedicated hardware.

4. **Cloud and Data Center Networks**: Virtual fabrics are heavily used in **cloud environments** (such as AWS, Google Cloud, or Microsoft Azure) and **modern data centers**. These environments often use virtual fabrics to support multi-tenant networks, network segmentation, and secure connectivity between virtual machines or containers.

5. **Network Function Virtualization (NFV)**: NFV is a key enabler of virtual fabrics. It virtualizes network functions (like load balancers, firewalls, and routers) that previously required dedicated hardware, enabling them to run in virtualized environments.

### Benefits of Virtual Fabrics:
- **Scalability**: Virtual fabrics can scale easily without requiring physical changes to the underlying hardware.
- **Flexibility**: Network services and configurations can be dynamically modified and provisioned via software, making it easier to adapt to changing requirements.
- **Cost-Effectiveness**: Virtual fabrics reduce the need for specialized networking hardware, as most networking functions can be performed by software running on general-purpose servers.
- **Automation**: The use of SDN in virtual fabrics enables the automation of network provisioning and management, reducing human intervention and the potential for errors.

### Examples of Virtual Fabric Solutions:
- **VMware NSX**: A popular virtual network fabric used in data centers and cloud environments, allowing the creation of virtual networks with security, routing, and network virtualization functions.
- **Cisco ACI Virtual Edge**: A solution that extends the capabilities of Cisco ACI to virtual environments, allowing for fully virtualized network fabric deployments.
- **Microsoft Azure Virtual Network (VNet)**: Provides network isolation and segmentation in the Azure cloud, essentially creating a virtual fabric for cloud resources.
- **AWS Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)**: AWS's network virtualization offering, which allows the creation of isolated virtual networks for workloads running in the AWS cloud.

### Conclusion:
A **virtual fabric** can be completely software-based, operating independently of physical networking infrastructure. It is widely used in cloud computing and modern data centers to provide scalable, flexible, and automated network management. By abstracting the network layer from hardware, virtual fabrics support dynamic, programmable, and software-driven network configurations that are ideal for large-scale and complex environments【10†source】【11†source】.

######  【13†Blog】 > 【26†News & Analysis】  | November 4, 2022 

###### TAGS: 【27†automation】 | 【28†Kubernetes】

# Hedgehog, Kubernetes, And The Network Automation Conundrum

###### 【29†Drew Conry-Murray】

This post originally appeared in Human Infrastructure, the Packet Pushers’ weekly newsletter. See 【30†back issues and sign up here】 to get it.

* * *

The networking startup 【31†Hedgehog†githedgehog.com】 recently emerged from stealth with a network fabric that brings together the open-source 【32†SONiC†github.com】 network OS (NOS) and the Kubernetes orchestration platform. The goal is to provide a distributed L3 network fabric (think edge, colocation, and cloud deployments) that can run on whitebox switches, SmartNICs/DPUs, and CPUs.

While Hedgehog is building its solution around SONiC and Kubernetes, the real value proposition is what these two elements aim to make possible: a network fabric that’s designed to be adaptable, software-driven, and automation-friendly. And because Hedgehog’s software can be deployed on a variety of hardware types, the startup can bring network connectivity to edge locations, data centers, and the cloud.

## Why Kubernetes?

It’s easy to understand why Hedgehog would choose SONiC for its NOS. As an open-source project it’s freely available to Hedgehog. More importantly, SONiC comes with robust community support from heavy hitters such as Microsoft and NVIDIA. Microsoft originally developed SONiC for use in its Azure cloud, and NVIDIA 【33†offers†www.nvidia.com】 switches and DPUs that can run the NOS.

So why throw Kubernetes into the mix? For one, SONiC is a containerized NOS. Individual elements of the NOS run as 【34†Docker containers†infohub.delltechnologies.com】 (for instance, there’s an SNMP container, a BGP container, and so on). Kubernetes is a container orchestration platform, so there’s a natural fit using Kubernetes to orchestrate your distributed SONiC nodes.

For another, Hedgehog is targeting organizations that are embracing modern development and operations practices, such as Web-scale companies, financial services, and very large enterprises. These organizations may already be using Kubernetes to run applications and services. Hedgehog aims to slot right into these organizations’ existing tools and processes.

“We turn Kubernetes into a network controller,” said Mark Austin, CEO and co-founder, in an interview. “We give customers a tool they can use to design a Hedgehog data center that then drives configuration of Hedgehog SONiC instances. Network functions can run in containers so we can compose or orchestrate a SONiC image, and then automate the configuration of any given network device for its role in the fabric.”

“We want to close the gap between DevOps, SRE, and NetOps,” he said.

Besides the NOS, Hedgehog provides a fabric operator to apply configurations to the SONiC nodes, set up BGP, create rules, and so on. There’s also a fabric designer, which is provided via SaaS. The fabric designer lets customers diagram and model the entire fabric in the cloud–including racks, switches, and DPUs–and then download seed images to the network devices, which will bring up those images and join the Kubernetes cluster.

## The Network Automation Conundrum

There’s a problem for Hedgehog and other networking companies looking to draft off the current enthusiasm for DevOps/NetOps/GitOps, Kubernetes, and the like. That problem is the network automation conundrum.

The 5 minutes you might save via automation requires many, many hours to build a reliable automation process. A reliable automation process is more than just a collection of scripts. You need telemetry and a source of truth. You need testing and code review to make sure the 【35†changes or tasks you hope to automate don’t destroy the network†blog.cloudflare.com】. You need a commit process to track who is making changes and why. You need training on new tools and products. Most importantly, you need buy-in from everyone on the team about all the new processes and pipelines.

This is not to say automation is bad or unfeasible. 【36†It’s not.†blog.ipspace.net】 Over time, the 5-minute jobs you automate add up to significant efficiencies. Even better, the testing and validation processes in your automation pipeline should reduce the problems that get introduced by human error. But it’s hard to get busy engineers to justify the time spent learning and building a reliable automation process when they can clear those blinking-red tasks by hand right now.

Hedgehog shouldn’t be expected to solve this problem. It’s not a problem any vendor can solve. But I’m not convinced you can put a Kubernetes wrapper over networking and then turn the network over to a DevOps team and expect a lot of success–and certainly not when something goes wrong. Kubernetes and DevOps is not a replacement for network engineers who know what they’re doing.

I wonder if Hedgehog is a little ahead of the curve in expecting to find a market of the right people with the right training and processes in place to take advantage of what the startup offers.

[Image 0] 

About Drew Conry-Murray: Drew Conry-Murray has been writing about information technology for more than 15 years, with an emphasis on networking, security, and cloud. He's co-host of 【37†The Network Break podcast】 and a Tech Field Day delegate. He loves real tea and virtual donuts, and is delighted that his job lets him talk with so many smart, passionate people. He writes 【38†novels†www.amazon.com】 in his spare time.

# The Benefits of Containers in SONiC

【12† Josh Saul 】 :  Mar 14, 2023 6:56:50 AM 

【13†Uncategorized】 

[Image 2: The Benefits of Containers in SONiC] 

The Benefits of Containers in SONiC - Hedgehog 

4:27

If you’re into network operating systems like me, then you’ll know that the containerization architecture of SONiC is a huge step forward for delivering on higher levels of network uptime.

In the past, most network operating systems were monolithic, which means they had a single codebase controlling all the network functions, one big fat image with all the features. But containerization changes all of that! Now, each network function is separated into its own container or microservice. It’s like having different rooms for different functions in your house instead of having everything in one big room. You can renovate a room completely while life in the other rooms remains unaffected.

Containers, microservices, and Kubernetes are the standards for deploying distributed applications in the cloud, perhaps it is time for networking to catch up? What can we expect with this new paradigm?

# Flexibility

[Image 3] 

Why are containers such a big deal? For starters, it gives us a lot more flexibility. With containerization, we can update or add new features to a specific service without having to change the entire operating system. This is a game-changer for configuring and troubleshooting networks. In the past, if there was a bug or vulnerability in a component that was not part of the data plane, you still needed to reboot the whole system in order to patch it. Sure there were proprietary solutions like ISSU, but they were somewhat unreliable and part of the vendor’s black box. With containers and open source software, we can be assured of which things are dependent on each other. This is a very important part of developing reliable systems.

# Scalability

[Image 4] 

Another big benefit is scalability. With containerization, we can scale individual services as needed instead of having to scale the entire system. This is great news for handling increasing network traffic without breaking the bank. We can scale up each individual container with additional resources, or spin up new identical containers to parallelize workloads to better match the necessary demand.

# Security

[Image 5] 

Security is also a major plus. Containerization provides an added layer of security for network operating systems. Each service is isolated in its own container, so any security breaches or vulnerabilities only affect that specific service, rather than the whole system. And if we find a piece of code that is insecure, we can replace it easily and quickly because we don’t need to replace the entire OS. That’s peace of mind that’s priceless!

# Efficiency

[Image 6] 

Efficiency is also a big selling point. Containerization allows each service to use only the resources it needs, which leads to cost savings, especially in large-scale networks. And the best part is that SONiC’s containerized approach allows for greater openness and interoperability with other systems. In the same way we might throttle inbound traffic to the management interfaces, we should also set limits on the management processes to ensure that they don’t blow up our control plane. The more control we have over system processes the more space we have available for the new and exciting pieces of software that we’ve always wanted to leverage in our network management.

# Orchestration

[Image 7] 

And here’s a bit of magic that might make you think differently about the future of networking. If the NOS is a collection of containers running our network services on nodes, and the network fabric is a group of these worker nodes participating in delivering a specific outcome, isn’t that basically the same thing as a distributed application? (NOD YOUR HEAD)

So can we use Kubernetes to deploy and operate these network services in an extremely intelligent fashion? You bet! We don’t need any proprietary network management system (NMS) or network controller, we can use Kubernetes and get all of the benefits of the entire Cloud Native community of tools to dramatically improve our capabilities.

## This is game changing.

Overall, I think containerization in SONiC is a huge improvement over monolithic network operating systems. It’s a more flexible, scalable, secure, efficient, and open approach. It’s exactly what application developers want when designing modern apps. The time has come for the network to catch up with the rest of modern infrastructure.

Till next time…

[Image 8]

# Flexibility

[Image 3] 

Why are containers such a big deal? For starters, it gives us a lot more flexibility. With containerization, we can update or add new features to a specific service without having to change the entire operating system. This is a game-changer for configuring and troubleshooting networks. In the past, if there was a bug or vulnerability in a component that was not part of the data plane, you still needed to reboot the whole system in order to patch it. Sure there were proprietary solutions like ISSU, but they were somewhat unreliable and part of the vendor’s black box. With containers and open source software, we can be assured of which things are dependent on each other. This is a very important part of developing reliable systems.

# Scalability

[Image 4] 

Another big benefit is scalability. With containerization, we can scale individual services as needed instead of having to scale the entire system. This is great news for handling increasing network traffic without breaking the bank. We can scale up each individual container with additional resources, or spin up new identical containers to parallelize workloads to better match the necessary demand.

# Security

[Image 5] 

Security is also a major plus. Containerization provides an added layer of security for network operating systems. Each service is isolated in its own container, so any security breaches or vulnerabilities only affect that specific service, rather than the whole system. And if we find a piece of code that is insecure, we can replace it easily and quickly because we don’t need to replace the entire OS. That’s peace of mind that’s priceless!

# Efficiency

[Image 6] 

Efficiency is also a big selling point. Containerization allows each service to use only the resources it needs, which leads to cost savings, especially in large-scale networks. And the best part is that SONiC’s containerized approach allows for greater openness and interoperability with other systems. In the same way we might throttle inbound traffic to the management interfaces, we should also set limits on the management processes to ensure that they don’t blow up our control plane. The more control we have over system processes the more space we have available for the new and exciting pieces of software that we’ve always wanted to leverage in our network management.

# Orchestration

[Image 7] 

And here’s a bit of magic that might make you think differently about the future of networking. If the NOS is a collection of containers running our network services on nodes, and the network fabric is a group of these worker nodes participating in delivering a specific outcome, isn’t that basically the same thing as a distributed application? (NOD YOUR HEAD)

So can we use Kubernetes to deploy and operate these network services in an extremely intelligent fashion? You bet! We don’t need any proprietary network management system (NMS) or network controller, we can use Kubernetes and get all of the benefits of the entire Cloud Native community of tools to dramatically improve our capabilities.

## This is game changing.

Overall, I think containerization in SONiC is a huge improvement over monolithic network operating systems. It’s a more flexible, scalable, secure, efficient, and open approach. It’s exactly what application developers want when designing modern apps. The time has come for the network to catch up with the rest of modern infrastructure.

Till next time…

[Image 8]

parts: but still though, an entirely software-based virtual fabric running on a simple server still needs physical routers and switches, yes or no? How does that work.

Yes, an entirely software-based virtual fabric running on a server still requires **physical routers and switches** to connect to the broader network and handle the physical movement of data packets between different servers, devices, or locations. The way it works involves layering **virtual networking** on top of the physical infrastructure.

### How It Works:
1. **Physical Underlay**: The underlying physical network is made up of traditional routers, switches, and network cables that provide the basic connectivity and transport for data. These physical devices create the "underlay" network, which handles packet forwarding, routing, and the movement of traffic across physical locations.

2. **Virtual Overlay**: On top of this physical network, a **virtual network fabric (overlay)** is created using technologies such as **VXLAN**, **NVGRE**, or **Geneve**. These protocols encapsulate the network traffic, allowing virtual machines (VMs), containers, or software-defined functions to communicate as if they were on the same local network, even if they're spread across different physical locations.

3. **Encapsulation and Tunneling**: The virtual network (overlay) works by encapsulating packets from virtual machines or containers into an encapsulation protocol (like VXLAN). These encapsulated packets are then sent over the physical network (underlay), using physical switches and routers to route the traffic between servers and data centers. Once they reach their destination, the packets are decapsulated and delivered to the correct virtual endpoint.

4. **Virtual Switches and Routers**: Within the servers, software-based switches and routers (like **Open vSwitch** or **NSX Edge routers**) perform the same functions as physical networking devices, but in a virtualized manner. These virtual network devices route traffic between different VMs or containers and manage network segmentation, firewall rules, and load balancing—all in software.

5. **Physical Network’s Role**: The physical network still plays an essential role in ensuring that traffic can flow between different servers and between different data centers. For example:
   - **Top-of-Rack (ToR) switches** connect servers to the network.
   - **Leaf-and-Spine architectures** are commonly used to provide scalable, low-latency interconnections in data centers.
   - **WAN routers** enable communication across geographic locations.

### Example:
Imagine a **cloud environment** where multiple servers are spread across different data centers. Each server may have several virtual machines (VMs) running on it, connected by a virtual fabric using VXLAN. The physical network’s routers and switches ensure that when VM1 on server A needs to send traffic to VM2 on server B, the physical network transports that traffic between the servers. At the same time, the virtual fabric ensures that both VMs are logically connected, as if they were on the same network segment, even if physically they are far apart.

### Conclusion:
While the **virtual network fabric** can handle logical networking functions like routing and switching in software, it **still depends on the physical underlay infrastructure** (routers, switches, and physical links) to move data packets between machines. The physical network provides the transport layer, while the virtual fabric adds the abstraction layer that allows for scalable, flexible, and programmable networking.
are there any virtual fabric topologies that do not use physical switches?

In practice, it is not feasible to have a fully virtual fabric topology that completely eliminates the need for physical switches. Here's why:

### The Role of Physical Switches:
- **Data Transmission**: Even in a highly virtualized network, physical switches are necessary for **moving data between physical servers**. Virtual machines (VMs) and containers rely on underlying hardware for connectivity, which means physical switches are required to carry data from one physical machine to another.
  
- **Transport Layer**: Virtual fabrics operate as overlays on top of a physical infrastructure, and the encapsulated packets generated by virtual networks (such as using VXLAN) still need to travel across the physical network.

### The Virtual-Only Scenarios:
While physical switches are always part of the broader network, in very **small, localized virtual environments**, you could have an entirely virtual fabric within a single server or hyper-converged infrastructure setup, where:
- **Virtual Switches** like **Open vSwitch (OVS)** can operate inside a single physical host, connecting VMs or containers running on that host.
- **Internal Routing**: Network communication happens purely within the software layer, without leaving the server.

However, in this scenario, the virtual fabric is extremely limited in scope (just one server or a few interconnected servers in the same rack), and external communication or scaling would still rely on physical switches and routers to transmit data beyond the host. 

### Conclusion:
Virtual fabrics without physical switches are limited to internal, localized communication within a single machine or small hyper-converged system. For anything beyond that, **physical switches remain necessary** to transmit data across the broader network, making it impossible to have a truly switch-less virtual fabric in larger networked environments.

oh btw, what sort of throughput do the physical toprack switches and WAN routers provide for virtual fabric servers to intercommunicate these days?

The throughput provided by **Top-of-Rack (ToR) switches** and **WAN routers** for virtual fabric servers varies depending on the specific hardware, but modern equipment typically offers very high throughput to meet the demands of virtualized environments, cloud infrastructure, and data centers.

### 1. **Top-of-Rack (ToR) Switches**:
   - **Common Throughput**: ToR switches today generally offer port speeds of **10 Gbps, 25 Gbps, 40 Gbps, 100 Gbps**, and even **400 Gbps** per port, depending on the model and vendor. For example:
     - **10/25/40 Gbps** is common in many large-scale data centers for inter-server communication.
     - **100 Gbps** is becoming standard for high-performance environments, especially in hyperscale data centers like those run by Amazon, Google, and Microsoft.
     - **400 Gbps** ports are emerging for next-generation data center needs.
   - **Backplane Capacity**: High-end switches have **backplane capacities** exceeding multiple terabits per second (Tbps) to support all active ports simultaneously at maximum throughput.
   - **Vendors**: Major vendors like Cisco, Arista, and Juniper offer ToR switches with high throughput to match the needs of modern data centers.

### 2. **WAN Routers**:
   - **Common Throughput**: WAN routers provide the backbone connectivity between geographically distributed data centers or cloud regions. Modern WAN routers often support **10 Gbps to 100 Gbps** interfaces, and in some cases, **400 Gbps** and beyond.
   - **Multi-Terabit Performance**: High-end routers, such as those from Cisco, Juniper, and Nokia, can support throughput in the **multiple terabits per second (Tbps)** range, ensuring that data can flow efficiently across long distances without bottlenecks.
   - **Carrier-Grade Routers**: These are often used by telecom providers and large enterprises. For instance, Cisco's **NCS 5500** series supports up to **16 Tbps** of total system throughput, making it suitable for connecting data centers and handling the traffic of large virtual fabric networks.

### Impact on Virtual Fabric Communication:
   - **Intra-Rack Communication**: High-throughput ToR switches ensure that virtualized servers within the same rack can communicate at extremely low latencies and high speeds, often using **10-100 Gbps** connections.
   - **Inter-Rack and Data Center Communication**: For virtual fabrics that span across multiple racks or even data centers, WAN routers and leaf-spine architectures ensure that traffic can flow seamlessly at **100 Gbps or higher** depending on the infrastructure.

### Conclusion:
Modern ToR switches and WAN routers are capable of handling massive amounts of traffic, with throughput speeds ranging from **10 Gbps** to **400 Gbps** per port and multi-terabit system capacities. This makes them well-suited for the high-bandwidth, low-latency demands of virtualized and containerized environments, ensuring smooth inter-server and inter-data-center communication in virtual fabric deployments.
what does backplane capacity mean?

**Backplane capacity** refers to the total data throughput that a network switch or router can handle internally, across all its ports, without any performance degradation. It represents the maximum amount of data that can travel simultaneously across the switch's or router's internal architecture, connecting all the ports together.

### Key Points:
1. **Internal Bandwidth**: Backplane capacity measures the aggregate internal bandwidth that the switch’s hardware can handle. For example, if a switch has 48 ports, each supporting 10 Gbps, the backplane must be capable of managing at least 480 Gbps (48 × 10 Gbps) to allow full-speed traffic on all ports at once.
   
2. **Non-Blocking vs. Blocking**:
   - **Non-blocking backplane**: A switch with a non-blocking backplane has enough capacity to allow full data throughput on all ports simultaneously without dropping packets or causing delays. In this case, the backplane capacity matches or exceeds the combined throughput of all ports.
   - **Blocking backplane**: A blocking switch has a backplane that cannot handle full simultaneous data flow across all ports, leading to potential bottlenecks if multiple ports are fully utilized at the same time.

3. **Importance**:
   - The higher the backplane capacity, the more efficient and reliable the switch is in handling high traffic loads, especially in data centers or networks where numerous devices communicate at high speeds.
   - Backplane capacity is a crucial factor in determining whether the switch can deliver **line-rate throughput**—that is, the ability to transmit data at the maximum capacity of each port without slowing down.

### Example:
- If a switch has 48 ports at 10 Gbps each, the ideal backplane capacity would be at least **960 Gbps** (48 ports × 10 Gbps in each direction, both incoming and outgoing), ensuring that traffic can flow in both directions across all ports simultaneously without any congestion.

In summary, **backplane capacity** is a critical performance metric that determines whether a switch or router can support maximum data flow across all its interfaces, particularly in high-demand environments like data centers and virtual fabrics.
do these high throughput switches still rely on exponential backoff for collision mitigation?

No, modern high-throughput network switches do **not rely on exponential backoff** for collision mitigation. Exponential backoff is a method used in older **Ethernet networks** operating in **half-duplex mode** with **CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)**, which was necessary when multiple devices shared a single medium (e.g., a coaxial cable) and collisions were common.

### Key Differences in Modern Switches:
1. **Full-Duplex Mode**: Modern high-throughput switches operate in **full-duplex mode**, which means that data can be sent and received simultaneously on each port without the risk of collisions. In full-duplex mode, each port has a dedicated communication path, so there is no need for collision detection or avoidance mechanisms like exponential backoff.

2. **Switching vs. Shared Medium**: In legacy Ethernet networks, devices shared the same transmission medium (such as a bus or hub), which led to collisions when multiple devices transmitted data at the same time. In contrast, **switches** create individual, isolated **collision domains** for each port. This means that each port operates independently, and devices connected to a switch do not share bandwidth or experience collisions with other devices.

3. **Higher Layer Flow Control**: Instead of relying on collision detection, modern switches use other techniques for managing traffic and congestion, such as:
   - **Flow control (IEEE 802.3x)**: Pause frames are used to control data flow and prevent buffer overflows at the receiving end.
   - **Quality of Service (QoS)**: Prioritizes certain types of traffic, ensuring that important data (like voice or video) gets through even under heavy network loads.
   - **Congestion control algorithms**: At higher layers (such as TCP), congestion control mechanisms are used to throttle the data flow when network congestion is detected.

### Modern Collision Mitigation:
- **CSMA/CD and Exponential Backoff** are specific to older **Ethernet networks** running in **half-duplex** mode. With the advent of **switched Ethernet** and **full-duplex communication**, these techniques are no longer needed.
- **Switched networks** provide dedicated communication paths between devices, which eliminates the possibility of collisions entirely.

### Conclusion:
Modern high-throughput switches use **full-duplex communication**, **dedicated links**, and advanced **flow control techniques** rather than relying on exponential backoff or collision detection. This allows them to handle large amounts of traffic without the need for collision mitigation at the physical layer.
how are the dedicated links physically possible in the hardware...how does that work for massive switches?

In modern network switches, **dedicated links** are physically possible due to a combination of advanced **hardware architecture** and high-speed **internal switching fabrics**. Here's how it works in large, high-throughput switches:

### 1. **Switching Fabric**:
   The internal **switching fabric** is the core of a switch, acting like the "backbone" that connects all the ports. The fabric allows multiple data paths to be established simultaneously between input and output ports, effectively creating **dedicated, parallel connections** for each communication session. In large-scale switches, this fabric is highly scalable, with capacities ranging from **terabits to petabits per second**.

   - **Crossbar Switches**: Many high-end switches use **crossbar switching fabric**, which is a grid of paths that can dynamically connect any input port to any output port. Crossbar designs allow for multiple concurrent connections without blocking, making it possible for data to flow between ports simultaneously without interference.

   - **Non-blocking architecture**: In modern switches, **non-blocking** architectures mean that the internal fabric has enough bandwidth to allow all ports to communicate at their maximum speeds at the same time. For example, if all ports are operating at 100 Gbps, the backplane must support the combined total of that throughput.

### 2. **Full-Duplex Communication**:
   Modern switches operate in **full-duplex mode**, meaning that each port can send and receive data simultaneously. This is possible because each port is connected to a dedicated circuit in the switch fabric. The data paths are set up dynamically by the switching fabric as packets enter and leave the switch.
